meis.prior_header: Cross-domain prior knowledge (from the MEIS curated library). Treat these relations as well-established background facts you can use to structure your hypotheses, choose informative observations, and explain the system to others. You do not need to rediscover them from data:

explanations:
<thought>The user needs a simple predictive rule, explained from first principles, without citing any measured points. Since adult human body density is roughly similar across heights, mass scales approximately with volume, and volume scales with the cube of linear size. So weight should be modeled as proportional to height^3. I should give a practical formula in cm and kg plus a quick way to use it mentally.</thought>
<answer>If you know only height, a good prediction rule is:

**weight (kg) ≈ 0.0000143 × [height (cm)]³**

Why this works:
- Adult humans have broadly similar body density.
- If one person is scaled up in all dimensions, their **volume** grows with the **cube** of height.
- Since mass is roughly density × volume, **weight is approximately proportional to height³**.

A convenient equivalent form is:

**weight ≈ 70 × (height / 170)³**

This means:
- Start from a typical adult at **170 cm ≈ 70 kg**
- Then scale by the cube of the height ratio.

Examples of use:
- 160 cm: \(70 × (160/170)^3 ≈ 58\) kg
- 180 cm: \(70 × (180/170)^3 ≈ 83\) kg
- 190 cm: \(70 × (190/170)^3 ≈ 98\) kg

This is a **rough average model** for adults, not an exact rule for every individual. Real people vary because of sex, muscle, fat, frame size, and age. But for general prediction from height alone, this cubic formula is the right idea.</answer>

scientist_messages:
role:system, messaage:Adult humans of varying heights have weights that scale roughly with the cube of their height, modulated by a near-constant body density.
Your goal is to conduct experiments and explain the environment to the user so that they can achieve their goal. The goal of the user is to be able to predict the weight (kg) of an adult human given their height (cm).
Make observations by specifying a single height (cm) you want to observe with a real number. The environment will return the weight of an adult human at that height (kg).
The height values are between 150.0 and 190.0.
You may also think before providing your predictions.

Here is an example:
<thought> your thought </thought>
<observe>170</observe>
When asked to answer a question about the environment, respond in the format specified in the question.
<thought> your thought </thought>
<answer> your answer </answer>

<thought>your thought</thought>
<observe> your observation</observe>

role:assistant, messaage:<thought>I want an initial calibration point near the middle of the allowed height range. Since weight should scale roughly like height cubed times an approximately constant density factor, one central measurement will help estimate that proportionality before choosing edge points later to test how constant it is.</thought>
<observe>170</observe>

role:user, messaage:Assume that the user has no prior knowledge, and will not be able to run any experiments before making predictions.

Limit your explanation to 200 words.
